content.text:
Looking at this problem, I need to build a regex that:
1. Matches a YYYY-MM-DD date only on lines that also contain an IPv4 address
2. Captures only the last date on the line
3. Validates date ranges (Feb up to 29) and IPv4 octets (no leading zeros)
4. Enforces non-alphanumeric boundaries

Let me verify the target directory and then construct/test the regex.

total 12
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Jun 30 23:56 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Jun 30 23:55 ..
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Jun 30 23:56 .atelier

tool_use_result.text:
total 12
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Jun 30 23:56 .
drwxr-xr-x 1 root root 4096 Jun 30 23:55 ..
drwxr-xr-x 3 root root 4096 Jun 30 23:56 .atelier

content.text: Let me build and test the regex with a Python script before saving.

input.command:
cat > /tmp/test_regex.py << 'PY'
import re

IPV4 = r"(?<![A-Za-z0-9.])(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|1\d\d|[1-9]?\d)(?:\.(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|1\d\d|[1-9]?\d)){3}(?![A-Za-z0-9.])"
DATE = r"(?<![A-Za-z0-9])(\d{4}-(?:(?:0[13578]|1[02])-(?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|3[01])|(?:0[469]|11)-(?:0[1-9]|[12]\d|30)|02-(?:0[1-9]|[12]\d)))(?![A-Za-z0-9])"

pattern = r"^(?=.*" + IPV4 + r").*" + DATE

log = """\
2020-01-15 connection from 192.168.0.1 ok
user 1134-12-1234 logged in from 10.0.0.1
event at 2021-03-10 then 2022-12-31 from 8.8.8.8 done
no ip here 2023-05-05 should not match
ip 256.1.1.1 invalid 2020-06-06 should not match
ip 1.2.3.4 with invalid date 2020-13-40 only
feb test 2024-02-29 from 1.1.1.1
feb invalid 2024-02-30 from 1.1.1.1
leading zero ip 192.168.0.01 date 2020-07-07
abc2020-08-08 glued ip 10.0.0.5
2020-08-08abc glued date ip 10.0.0.5
multiple 2019-01-01 2019-02-02 2019-03-03 ip 172.16.0.1
date 2020-04-31 invalid 30day ip 5.5.5.5
"""

matches = re.findall(pattern, log, re.MULTILINE)
print("MATCHES:", matches)

expected = [
    "2020-01-15",   # line1
    "2022-12-31",   # line3 last date
    "2024-02-29",   # feb 29 ok
    # feb 30 -> no date match (no valid date) -> line skipped
    "2019-03-03",   # last of multiple
]
print("EXPECTED-ish:", expected)
PY
python3 /tmp/test_regex.py

content.text:
MATCHES: ['2020-01-15', '2022-12-31', '2024-02-29', '2019-03-03']
EXPECTED-ish: ['2020-01-15', '2022-12-31', '2024-02-29', '2019-03-03']

[discipline] Prefer the `code_search` tool over shell `cat` for code exploration.

content.text: All test cases pass correctly. Now saving the regex to the file.

Saved to `/app/regex.txt` and verified. The regex works as required:

```
^(?=.*<IPv4>).*<DATE>
```

**How it works:**
- `^(?=.*<IPv4>)` — a line-anchored lookahead requiring the line to contain a valid IPv4 address anywhere. The IPv4 part `(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|1\d\d|[1-9]?\d)(?:\.…){3}` rejects octets >255 and leading zeros, bounded by `(?<![A-Za-z0-9.])`/`(?![A-Za-z0-9.])` so it isn't part of a longer alphanumeric/dotted token.
- `.*(<DATE>)` — the greedy `.*` forces the captured group to be the **last** date on the line. Date validation handles 31-/30-day months and February ≤29, with `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])`/`(?![A-Za-z0-9])` boundaries to avoid false matches like `1134-12-1234`.
- The date is the only capturing group, so `re.findall` returns just the date string per matching line.

**Verified behavior:** lines with no IPv4 are skipped; invalid IPv4s (`256.x`, leading zeros) don't qualify; invalid dates (`2020-13-40`, `2024-02-30`, `2020-04-31`) produce no match; glued tokens (`abc2020-08-08`, `1134-12-1234`) are rejected; and only the last date is returned when several appear.

result:
Saved to `/app/regex.txt` and verified. The regex works as required:

```
^(?=.*<IPv4>).*<DATE>
```

**How it works:**
- `^(?=.*<IPv4>)` — a line-anchored lookahead requiring the line to contain a valid IPv4 address anywhere. The IPv4 part `(?:25[0-5]|2[0-4]\d|1\d\d|[1-9]?\d)(?:\.…){3}` rejects octets >255 and leading zeros, bounded by `(?<![A-Za-z0-9.])`/`(?![A-Za-z0-9.])` so it isn't part of a longer alphanumeric/dotted token.
- `.*(<DATE>)` — the greedy `.*` forces the captured group to be the **last** date on the line. Date validation handles 31-/30-day months and February ≤29, with `(?<![A-Za-z0-9])`/`(?![A-Za-z0-9])` boundaries to avoid false matches like `1134-12-1234`.
- The date is the only capturing group, so `re.findall` returns just the date string per matching line.

**Verified behavior:** lines with no IPv4 are skipped; invalid IPv4s (`256.x`, leading zeros) don't qualify; invalid dates (`2020-13-40`, `2024-02-30`, `2020-04-31`) produce no match; glued tokens (`abc2020-08-08`, `1134-12-1234`) are rejected; and only the last date is returned when several appear.
